deck.title: Onboarding
deck.tagline: Seven modules. From expectations to execution. Select a topic to begin.
deck.introSubtitle: Team guidebook for AI-assisted delivery workflows

expectations.title: Expectations & Standards
expectations.subtitle: What every team member must know on day one.
expectations.headline: Team Expectations
expectations.subheadline: AI accelerates us — humans govern the outcome.
expectations.banner: Every team member is responsible for the quality, security, and correctness of the work they produce — regardless of whether AI assisted in creating it. AI is a tool, not a teammate. You own the output.

cards.title: You Own Every Line
cards.body: AI-generated code ships under your name. You are accountable for understanding, reviewing, and validating every artifact before it leaves your workstation — no exceptions.

cards.title: No Sensitive Data in AI
cards.body: Never paste CUI, PII, credentials, API keys, or any data classified above public into any AI tool. This is a hard stop. Violations are reportable incidents.

cards.title: AI-Modified Sprints
cards.body: We run 1-week sprints with AI acceleration baked in. Higher velocity expectations but more frequent checkpoints. Every MR goes through human review.

cards.title: Complete Tickets Required
cards.body: AI tools produce better output from better input. Tickets must have fully fleshed out acceptance criteria, technical context, and data models before development begins.

expectations.callout: Speed without governance is recklessness. Governance without speed is irrelevance. We do both.

tools.title: Tools & Guardrails
tools.subtitle: What's approved, what's restricted, and where the lines are.
tools.headline: Approved Tools & Boundaries

approved.title: GitHub Copilot
approved.desc: Primary dev tool and general-use AI for experienced users. Autocomplete, inline chat, planning mode, agent mode, sub-agents — all in VS Code.

approved.title: Sidekick
approved.desc: General-purpose AI assistant across all roles. Custom Skills development and integrations for team-specific workflows.

approved.title: M365 Copilot
approved.desc: Full Office suite — Word (doc drafting), PowerPoint (decks), Excel (data analysis), Designer (visuals), Outlook, Teams.

approved.title: VS Code
approved.desc: Primary IDE for AI-assisted workflows. Planning mode, agent mode, sub-agent orchestration, MCP server integration.

approved.title: Visual Studio
approved.desc: .NET / Razor Pages development. Copilot available but agent/planning mode features differ from VS Code — know the differences.

forbidden.title: Loading CUI/FOUO Data
forbidden.desc: Never input Controlled Unclassified Information into any AI tool — cloud-hosted or local. Full stop.

forbidden.title: PII in Prompts
forbidden.desc: No real names, SSNs, case numbers, beneficiary data, or any personally identifiable information in any AI context.

forbidden.title: Production Credentials
forbidden.desc: Never paste API keys, tokens, passwords, connection strings, or secrets into AI tools. Use placeholders.

forbidden.title: Unapproved Tools
forbidden.desc: No personal free-tier AI tools for work tasks. No browser extensions that send code to unknown endpoints.

forbidden.title: Blind Copy-Paste
forbidden.desc: Never paste AI output into production without reading, understanding, and testing it first. You own it.

tools.callout: The tools are powerful. The guardrails are non-negotiable. Internalize both.

devworkflow.title: Developer Workflow
devworkflow.subtitle: From ticket to merge request — the full AI-assisted loop.
devworkflow.headline: The AI-Assisted Development Loop
devworkflow.subheadline: Each step has a human checkpoint. AI accelerates — it never decides.

steps.title: Ticket Requirements
steps.body: Start with a fully fleshed out ticket. Acceptance criteria must be specific, testable, and include edge cases. Include data models, API contracts, and UI mockups where applicable.
steps.tip: If the ticket isn't complete, push it back. Incomplete tickets produce incomplete AI output.

steps.title: Planning Mode
steps.body: Use VS Code Copilot planning mode to develop a detailed implementation plan. Feed it ticket requirements, architecture docs, coding standards, and relevant codebase context.
steps.tip: Have it probe for edge cases, integration risks, and missing requirements. The plan is the product — code gen is just execution.

steps.title: Sub-Agent Execution
steps.body: Break the plan into granular tasks. Use multiple models and specialized personas: a code generator, a test writer, a reviewer. Each sub-agent operates on a well-scoped piece of the plan.
steps.tip: Match model to task strength. Use Sidekick Skills and MCP servers to encode team conventions.

steps.title: Unit Test Generation
steps.body: Generate unit tests alongside implementation. Cover happy paths, edge cases, error handling, and boundary conditions.
steps.tip: Pattern: 'Write unit tests for [function]. Cover: happy path, null input, boundary values, error states. Use [framework]. Mock [dependencies].'

steps.title: Integration & E2E Tests
steps.body: Generate Playwright tests for critical user flows on Razor Pages front-end. Validate selectors against actual UI. Include in GitLab CI/CD pipeline.
steps.tip: Have AI read the Razor Page code first. Devs can also use Playwright to investigate how defects present to end users.

steps.title: Multi-Angle Code Review
steps.body: Run AI-assisted code reviews from multiple perspectives before the human MR review: correctness, security/vulnerability, performance, and adversarial/antagonistic review.
steps.tip: Adversarial prompt: 'You are a hostile code reviewer. Find every way this could fail, leak data, or be exploited. Be ruthless.'

steps.title: MR Creation & Review
steps.body: Create a clean merge request with AI-generated descriptions. Human reviewer validates against AC, checks for hallucinated imports and phantom APIs, and approves.
steps.tip: MR description: What changed, why, how tested, what could break, rollback plan. AI drafts — you verify.

devworkflow.callout: The loop is tight by design. Every AI output passes through a human gate before it touches the codebase.

disciplines.title: Engineering Disciplines
disciplines.subtitle: Context engineering, prompt craft, tooling, and documentation.
disciplines.headline: Core Engineering Disciplines

pillars.title: Context Engineering
items: Curate what the model sees — architecture docs, coding standards, data models, relevant code
Use system prompts and project-level instructions to steer output
Manage context window budget — prioritize signal over noise
Build reusable context packs per project area

pillars.title: Prompt Engineering
items: Standardized prompt templates for recurring tasks
Version-control prompts alongside code — they are engineering artifacts
Use structured output formats (XML, JSON schemas)
Iterate prompts like code: test, measure, refine

pillars.title: Tool & Skill Dev
items: Build MCP servers for structured access to internal services
Create custom Sidekick Skills encoding team conventions
Develop reusable tool chains — composable capabilities
Evaluate tools: Copilot for code gen, Sidekick for reasoning, M365 Copilot for docs

disciplines.callout: AI can draft ADR, ICD, IMS, SDD, SIP, changelogs, and onboarding guides — but a human must validate accuracy before any doc is baselined.

qa.title: QA & Testing
qa.subtitle: How front-end QA testers leverage AI for testing at speed.
qa.headline: QA Testers & AI
qa.subheadline: From manual testing to AI-assisted test automation.
qa.banner: Front-end QA testers can use AI to learn and write Playwright tests for our Razor Pages front-end, augment manual testing, and contribute directly to our CI/CD pipeline.

cards.title: Learning Playwright with AI
cards.body: Use AI as a tutor: paste a Razor Page component and ask it to explain how to write a Playwright test. Start simple, then build to form interactions and visual regression.

cards.title: Tests in the Pipeline
cards.body: AI-generated Playwright tests go into the CI/CD pipeline. Every automated test is one less manual regression cycle. Focus on critical happy paths first.

cards.title: Augmenting Manual Testing
cards.body: Use AI to generate test case matrices from acceptance criteria, identify edge cases, and draft defect reports with reproduction steps.

cards.title: Test Coverage Mapping
cards.body: AI maps test cases back to requirements for traceability. Feed it your AC and test suite — it identifies coverage gaps faster than manual review.

qa.callout: QA doesn't need to become engineering. But QA that speaks the language of automation multiplies the whole team's velocity.

functional.title: Functional Team
functional.subtitle: RTM, use cases, roadmaps, and documentation — AI's role and limits.
functional.headline: Functional Team AI Use Cases

approved.title: RTM Development
approved.desc: Draft RTMs from user stories and test cases. M365 Copilot in Excel for structured data, Sidekick for narrative. Human validation mandatory.

approved.title: Use Case Documentation
approved.desc: Generate use case templates and exception flows. M365 Copilot in Word for drafting. You own the content.

approved.title: Product Roadmap Drafting
approved.desc: Synthesize backlog and sprint outcomes into roadmap narratives. M365 Copilot in PowerPoint for presentations.

approved.title: Meeting Summaries
approved.desc: Structure notes into summaries, decisions, and action items. Approved tools only — never upload recordings with CUI.

approved.title: Process Documentation
approved.desc: SOPs, workflow diagrams, onboarding guides — AI drafts, humans refine. Version control all documentation.

forbidden.title: Loading CUI Documents
forbidden.desc: Never upload or paste CUI-marked documents, FOUO data, or controlled technical information into any AI tool.

forbidden.title: Client Data in Prompts
forbidden.desc: No beneficiary names, case data, claim numbers, or client-specific PII. Use sanitized examples.

forbidden.title: Deliverable Without Review
forbidden.desc: AI-drafted deliverables must be reviewed for accuracy and compliance before sharing with the client.

forbidden.title: Replacing Human Analysis
forbidden.desc: AI summarizes and structures — it doesn't analyze policy implications or assess risk. That's your job.

functional.callout: AI is your drafting assistant, not your analyst. It structures information — you validate meaning.

compliance.title: Data & Compliance
compliance.subtitle: CUI, PII, and the non-negotiable rules of data handling.
compliance.headline: Data Classification & AI Boundaries
compliance.subheadline: Know your data. Know your limits. No exceptions.

items.label: CUI / FOUO
items.desc: Controlled Unclassified Information — strictly prohibited from any AI tool.

items.label: PII
items.desc: Names, SSNs, dates of birth, addresses, case numbers, beneficiary data.

items.label: Credentials & Secrets
items.desc: API keys, tokens, passwords, connection strings, certificates, private keys.

items.label: Production Data
items.desc: Real database contents, production logs with user data, client system exports.

items.label: Internal Architecture Docs
items.desc: Sanitize system names and endpoints. Remove environment-specific details.

items.label: Ticket Descriptions
items.desc: Scrub client names, project codes, and specific data references first.

items.label: Code Patterns
items.desc: Generic patterns fine. Strip comments referencing client systems, file paths, config values.

items.label: Open Source Code
items.desc: Publicly available code, libraries, frameworks. No restrictions.

items.label: General Technical Questions
items.desc: Language features, framework patterns, best practices — all unrestricted.

items.label: Sanitized Examples
items.desc: Anonymized code with placeholder data, generic variable names.

items.label: Learning & Exploration
items.desc: New technologies, design patterns, skill building — use AI freely.

compliance.callout: When in doubt, don't paste it. Ask your lead. There is no 'undo' once data enters an AI model's context window.
